pair_convention.spot_explanation: CHF/JPY at 201.63 means 1 Swiss Franc buys 201.63 Japanese Yen. A rising CHF/JPY rate means CHF is strengthening relative to JPY (more yen per franc), while a falling rate means CHF is weakening relative to JPY (fewer yen per franc). The pair has surged approximately 21% from the March 2025 low of 166.09, driven by the Iran-war energy-asymmetry regime favouring CHF over JPY.

executive_summary: CHF/JPY trades at 201.63 as of May 9, 2026, near the 52-week high of 204.43 set on April 21 and in the 93rd percentile of the 52-week range, having surged approximately 21% from the March 2025 low of 166.09. The pair has appreciated 9.94% over the past year, driven by a structural regime in which the Swiss franc has functioned as the superior safe haven during the Iran-US war due to Switzerland's negligible energy import dependence, while the yen has been crippled by Japan's 95.9% Middle East crude oil dependency. Over the past month, CHF/JPY has declined 0.63% as Iran ceasefire negotiations and suspected MoF yen intervention on May 1 have partially reversed the CHF-over-JPY safe-haven premium. The bilateral carry is modest at -75bp (SNB 0.00% vs BoJ 0.75%) favouring JPY, but this is dwarfed by the structural fundamentals: Switzerland's 7% current-account surplus and AAA sovereign versus Japan's 250% debt-to-GDP and terms-of-trade vulnerability. Our stance is tactically Bearish CHF/JPY at conviction 2/5 — the BOJ's 6-3 hawkish dissent at the April 28 meeting, active ceasefire negotiations reducing CHF's safe-haven premium, and the pair's proximity to multi-decade highs all argue for downside pressure — but strategically Neutral at conviction 2/5 as the structural CHF advantages (fiscal, current account, inflation superiority) offset the cyclical BoJ normalisation over 1-6 months.

market_context.valuation_assessment: The CHF dossier notes CHF/JPY is roughly fairly valued on a PPP basis, with the implied fair value around 195-205 (CHF -2.5% deviation vs JPY). This is unusual for a CHF cross — CHF is deeply overvalued versus almost every other G10 currency — but JPY's structural undervaluation (40% below OECD PPP vs USD) largely offsets CHF's overvaluation, making the cross approximately fair. The pair sits near the upper end of PPP-implied fair value, suggesting limited upside on valuation grounds.

positioning.base_cftc_net: Asset managers net short -37,534 contracts; leveraged funds net short -5,174 contracts
positioning.net_pair_implication: Specs are positioned for CHF weakness (short CHF) AND JPY strength (long JPY) simultaneously, creating a structural flow bias toward CHF/JPY downside. The historic CHF short-squeeze risk is real on re-escalation, but the JPY long positioning reduces the pair's squeeze asymmetry compared to early 2025.
positioning.crowding_assessment: Pair trade moderately crowded on short side (specs bearish CHF/JPY); CHF asset-manager short at 95th percentile creates squeeze risk on re-escalation

pair_technicals.key_moving_averages: 50d SMA: 201.58, 100d SMA: 201.44, 200d SMA: 198.10 — bullish-stacked configuration but 50d and 100d converging within ~15 pips of spot, signalling momentum exhaustion
pair_technicals.chart_pattern: Rising wedge pattern with break below support line identified; range-bound consolidation near 52-week highs with bearish momentum; TradingView identifies resistance zone at 203.33-203.83 with bearish expectation toward 201.71 if that zone holds

pair_volatility.rv_vs_iv: Realised volatility has moderated significantly from the March 2025 war-peak acceleration (daily ranges exceeding 2-3 yen) to current daily ranges of approximately 1.0-1.5 yen; vol regime is transitional with RV below IV suggesting mild variance risk premium
pair_volatility.risk_reversals: Risk reversal data for CHF/JPY specifically not available; characterised directionally from component-currency risk reversals — CHF-call bid on USD/CHF and JPY-call bid on USD/JPY both suggest mild put demand on CHF/JPY
pair_volatility.vol_term_structure: Vol term structure expected to reprice around the June 16-18 BOJ/SNB meeting cluster; current structure likely flat-to-inverted reflecting near-term event risk
pair_volatility.vol_regime: Transitional — lower than wartime peaks but elevated versus pre-war grind; compressing from war-period highs

market_context.recent_regime: CHF/JPY has shifted from a powerful uptrend into a consolidation regime since early April. The pair tested a record area high of 204.43 on April 21 before pulling back sharply to 200.44 on April 30, coinciding with suspected MoF yen intervention. Iran ceasefire headlines on May 5-6 created further two-way volatility, with the pair initially declining on the premise that reduced geopolitical risk diminishes CHF's safe-haven premium relative to JPY. The dominant narrative has migrated from 'buy CHF/JPY on Iran escalation' to 'range-trade CHF/JPY pending ceasefire resolution and BOJ June decision.'

bilateral_carry_analysis.breakeven_move: Spot can move approximately 12 pips against before carry is erased over 4 weeks — trivially small relative to daily ranges of 100-200 pips
bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The bilateral carry on CHF/JPY is -75bp annualised (SNB at 0.00% vs BOJ at 0.75%), making CHF the lower-yielding currency. This is the smallest carry gap versus any G10 peer for CHF — negative but modest. The carry-to-vol ratio of approximately -0.083 (75bp divided by ~9% IV) is negligible, meaning carry is not a dominant driver in either direction. The breakeven move over 4 weeks is approximately 12 pips of CHF/JPY downside — trivially small relative to daily ranges. Carry regime is effectively neutral; the pair is driven by safe-haven relative value and energy-asymmetry, not carry differential. As the BOJ hikes toward 1.00% by Q3, the carry gap widens to -100bp, marginally increasing the headwind against CHF/JPY longs. If the SNB cuts to -0.25% in an extreme scenario, the carry gap would jump to -100bp or more, amplifying CHF/JPY bearishness.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The SNB-BOJ rate differential of -75bp (SNB 0.00% vs BOJ 0.75%) is the narrowest negative carry CHF faces against any G10 currency, but the trajectory is what matters: the BOJ's April 28 meeting delivered a 6-3 hawkish dissent — the most divided vote since 2016 — with three members favouring an immediate hike to 1.00%. Meanwhile the SNB held at 0.00% on March 19 with no prospect of hiking for the foreseeable future. The bilateral rate gap is directionally widening against CHF/JPY: the BOJ is moving toward 1.00% while the SNB is anchored at 0.00%, projecting the differential to -100bp by Q3. Swiss 10Y yields at 0.35% versus JGB 10Y at 2.50% create a -215bp sovereign spread, the widest in decades on the JGB side, reflecting BOJ normalisation and Takaichi fiscal stimulus. OIS-implied paths suggest approximately 10bp of BOJ June hike pricing versus a flat SNB path through 2026 — the asymmetry favours JPY more than CHF on rates. The June 17 BOJ decision coincides with the June 18 SNB decision, creating a binary cluster: if BOJ hikes and SNB holds, CHF/JPY could drop 300-500 pips on a single day.
key_bilateral_metrics: SNB policy rate: 0.00% vs BOJ: 0.75% = -75bp differential (widening to -100bp by Q3)
Swiss 10Y: 0.35% vs JGB 10Y: 2.50% = -215bp spread (widest since 1997 on the JGB side)
BOJ June hike probability: ~35-40% (3 hawkish dissenters at April meeting)
SNB June action: ~95% probability of hold at 0.00%
Bilateral carry annualised: -75bp; forward-implied depreciation: ~37bp per annum
tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices a slow BOJ normalisation with the SNB anchored. A June BOJ hike is under-priced given the three-way dissent; CHF/JPY would reprice 200-300 pips lower on delivery.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 1-6 months, the BOJ is expected to deliver 1-2 hikes reaching 1.00-1.25% by year-end (MUFG, Sony Financial forecasts) while the SNB remains at 0.00% or potentially cuts to -0.25% if EUR/CHF breaks 0.90. The projected end-2026 bilateral policy gap of -100 to -125bp represents a 25-50bp widening from current levels — directionally bearish CHF/JPY. The BOJ's Outlook Report raising FY2026 core CPI to 2.8% provides the fundamental justification for continued normalisation that the SNB lacks (Swiss CPI at just 0.6%). Structurally, both currencies share the funding-currency role, but the BOJ's exit from this status is faster and more credible than any potential SNB re-engagement with negative rates. The JGB curve's steepening (2s10s +145bp, steepest since 1990s) reflects a structural repricing of Japan's rate environment that has no parallel in Switzerland.
key_bilateral_metrics: Projected end-2026 SNB rate: 0.00% vs BOJ: 1.00-1.25% = -100 to -125bp gap
BOJ FY2026 core CPI projection: 2.8% vs Swiss April CPI: 0.6% = 220bp inflation differential
JGB 30Y: ~3.85% vs Swiss long-end: ~0.50% = -335bp spread at long end
Rate path divergence: BOJ hiking while SNB anchored or cutting
Forward-implied CHF/JPY 6M: ~200.88 (mild depreciation)
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price approximately one BOJ hike over 6M with the SNB on hold. We see 1.5-2 hikes as more likely, with the SNB potentially signalling negative-rate risk by September — both legs widen against CHF/JPY.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons bearish CHF/JPY; strategic confidence higher (70 vs 65) because the multi-meeting BOJ normalisation path provides more events for the rate-divergence thesis to materialise.

carry_analysis.breakeven_move: Approximately 12 pips of CHF/JPY downside over 4 weeks — trivially small relative to daily ranges
carry_analysis.carry_assessment: Carry is not a dominant driver; the -75bp differential is the smallest negative carry CHF faces in G10. As BOJ hikes toward 1.00%, the gap widens to -100bp, incrementally increasing headwinds for CHF/JPY longs.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral inflation picture creates an unusual dynamic. Swiss CPI at 0.6% with core at 0.3% makes CHF the cheapest real-yield currency in G10, while Japan's core CPI at 1.8% with core-core at 2.4% is above the BOJ's target. For CHF/JPY, this creates a tension: Swiss real yields are superior (higher after inflation adjustment given near-zero nominal rates), which supports CHF structurally, but the higher Japanese inflation is what forces the BOJ to normalise — the direct catalyst for JPY strength. Growth differentials are minor: Switzerland at 0.8% YoY vs Japan at 1.3% YoY (Q4 2025), with both economies slowing. Shunto wage growth at 5.09% in Japan anchors a domestic inflation floor that Switzerland lacks. The net tactical signal is balanced: Swiss real-yield superiority supports CHF, but Japanese inflation persistence supports the BOJ hawkish pivot that strengthens JPY.
key_bilateral_metrics: Swiss headline CPI: 0.6% vs Japan headline: 1.5% = -90bp gap
Swiss core CPI: 0.3% vs Japan core-core: 2.4% = -210bp gap
Swiss real 10Y: -0.25% vs Japan real 10Y: ~+0.70% (using core CPI) = -95bp real rate gap
Swiss GDP 2026 forecast: 1.0% vs Japan: 0.5% (BOJ) = Switzerland modestly stronger
Shunto 2026 wage growth: 5.09% (no Swiss equivalent above 1-2%)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price Swiss inflation staying low and Japanese inflation moderating. The bilateral real-yield comparison is roughly fair at current levels.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the inflation bifurcation creates offsetting forces. Swiss real-yield superiority persists as a structural CHF positive, but the BOJ's response to above-target inflation dominates via the rate channel. If Japan's core CPI stays above 2% through 2026 (our expectation), the BOJ normalisation path accelerates, widening the rate differential against CHF/JPY. If Swiss CPI undershoots 0% (possible if CHF stays strong and Iran resolves), the SNB's negative-rate conversation re-emerges — bearish CHF. Net: approximately balanced, hence Neutral.
key_bilateral_metrics: Swiss 2026 average CPI forecast: 0.5-0.7% vs Japan FY2026 BOJ forecast: 2.8%
Swiss growth forecast: 1.0% vs Japan: 0.5% (converging)
Probability of Swiss CPI <0%: 15-20% (if EUR/CHF stays <0.93)
Probability of Japan core CPI above 2%: 60-70% (Shunto anchoring)
Structural real-yield advantage CHF vs JPY: ~50-100bp (diminishing as BOJ hikes)
strategic.what_is_priced: The structural real-yield advantage of CHF is partially priced; the BOJ's response to above-target inflation is under-priced at the margin.

factors.horizon_split: Both Neutral; the inflation/growth dynamics create genuinely offsetting forces for this pair at both horizons.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: CHF/JPY is a safe-haven-vs-safe-haven cross where the energy-asymmetry regime makes risk appetite a dominant bilateral driver (+0.03 tactical / +0.02 strategic)

tactical.bilateral_analysis: This is the critical bilateral factor that has driven CHF/JPY's extraordinary rally. CHF/JPY is a safe-haven-versus-safe-haven cross, but in the Iran-war regime, the pair has behaved as a risk proxy because the energy shock asymmetry is decisive: Switzerland has negligible energy import exposure (5% CPI weight for energy) while Japan is 95.9% dependent on Middle East crude. TradingView analysis noted that Switzerland's low energy reliance 'heavily insulates the Swiss economy from the shock' while 'Japan is entirely exposed and completely reliant on imported crude.' The active ceasefire negotiations (May 5-6) are directly bearish CHF/JPY because they narrow the energy-asymmetry premium that has driven the pair from 166 to 204. If ceasefire firms and Brent reverts from $102 toward $80, Japan's terms of trade improve dramatically, strengthening JPY relative to CHF and pressuring CHF/JPY lower. The tactical risk-sentiment signal is bearish CHF/JPY because the dominant regime driver (Iran-war CHF premium) is actively reversing.
key_bilateral_metrics: Swiss CPI energy weight: ~5% vs Japan: ~20% = 15pp asymmetry
Japan ME crude dependence: 95.9% vs Switzerland: near-zero
Brent: ~$102/bbl (down from $120 wartime peak; ceasefire targeting $80)
Gold: $4,719 (+25% YTD — supports CHF safe-haven)
VIX: 17.13 (35th percentile — benign)
tactical.what_is_priced: Markets price approximately 50/50 odds on Iran ceasefire. If ceasefire firms, CHF/JPY downside of 500-800 pips is plausible as the energy asymmetry premium unwinds.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, if Iran resolves, JPY's classical safe-haven correlation reasserts (backed by $3.3T NIIP), making the two currencies more similar in safe-haven character. CHF/JPY would then revert to being driven primarily by rate differentials rather than energy asymmetry. Multiple geopolitical flashpoints over 6M (US-China, European fiscal stress, US debt ceiling) would affect both currencies similarly rather than asymmetrically, rendering the risk-sentiment factor Neutral for the pair.
key_bilateral_metrics: 6M probability of major risk-off event: ~50%
CHF beta to risk-off (5Y): +0.18-0.25 vs JPY: +0.15-0.30 (similar)
Energy-shock persistence probability: 25% (if ceasefire fails)
Japan NIIP: +$3.3T (structural repatriation channel if risk-off)
Switzerland C/A surplus: 7% of GDP (structural safe-haven anchor)
strategic.what_is_priced: The energy-asymmetry premium embedded in the current CHF/JPY level (~15-20 yen above pre-war equilibrium) is partially priced for normalisation.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Bearish, Strategic Neutral. The tactical signal reflects the active unwinding of the energy-asymmetry premium; the strategic signal reflects the normalisation of both currencies' safe-haven characters once Iran resolves.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral positioning setup is directionally bearish CHF/JPY. CFTC asset managers are net short CHF -37,534 contracts (95th percentile of 5Y history) while leveraged funds are net long JPY +56,400 contracts (75th percentile of 1Y). Netting these: specs are positioned for CHF weakness AND JPY strength simultaneously, creating a structural flow bias toward CHF/JPY downside. The critical contrarian observation: CHF's asset-manager short at the 95th percentile creates squeeze risk on a re-escalation (CHF/JPY could spike higher), but the JPY long positioning at +56,400 means the classic JPY short-squeeze catalyst has been exhausted. The net pair positioning is arguably mildly bearish — for CHF/JPY to rally significantly, BOTH legs need to move in the pair's favour (CHF squeeze + JPY unwind), which is a lower joint probability than either individually.
key_bilateral_metrics: CHF asset managers net: -37,534 contracts (95th percentile of 5Y history)
JPY leveraged funds net: +56,400 contracts (long, 75th percentile of 1Y)
Net pair positioning implication: bearish CHF/JPY (specs positioned for pair to fall)
CHF squeeze potential: 150+ pips on re-escalation (reference March 2026)
JPY long-unwind potential: 200-400 pips if BOJ disappoints
tactical.what_is_priced: The positioning asymmetry favours CHF/JPY downside modestly, but the joint probability of a squeeze (both currencies' positions unwinding toward pair upside) is lower than at the March 2026 peak.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, positioning normalises on both sides. CHF's structural funding-currency short gradually unwinds if stagflation persists; JPY's leveraged-fund long normalises as the carry trade potentially rebuilds (though at compressed differentials). Net positioning is Neutral strategically because the bilateral positioning dynamics are path-dependent on the BOJ's actual delivery.
key_bilateral_metrics: CHF net-short 6M normalisation: likely unwind to -25k from -37.5k
JPY net-long 6M trajectory: likely maintain 30-60k range if BOJ delivers
Combined positioning normalisation rate: moderate
Squeeze probability (6M window): 15-20%
Durable long-JPY accumulation probability: 40%
strategic.what_is_priced: Strategic positioning pricing is approximately fair.

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Bearish, Strategic Neutral. Positioning is a stronger signal at tactical horizons where weekly CFTC data provides actionable information.

tactical.combined_positioning_signal: Specs short CHF and long JPY simultaneously — net pair positioning bearish CHF/JPY at the 95th/75th percentile extremes respectively
tactical.unwind_trigger: Iran re-escalation forcing CHF squeeze while simultaneously triggering JPY terms-of-trade unwind; Brent spiking above $120 sustained
tactical.historical_analogue: March 2026: CHF/JPY rallied approximately 500 pips in two weeks on Iran-war escalation, squeezing CHF shorts
tactical.contrarian_case: CHF/JPY positioning is mildly stretched toward the downside with specs bearish the pair. The contrarian trigger for upside is an Iran re-escalation forcing a CHF short-squeeze while simultaneously triggering JPY terms-of-trade deterioration. Reference March 2026 when CHF/JPY rallied approximately 500 pips in two weeks. Current positioning could generate a similar magnitude move on a surprise re-escalation, but the probability is diminished with active ceasefire negotiations and requires both legs to move simultaneously against prevailing positions.

strategic.combined_positioning_signal: Positioning normalises over 6 months; contrarian signal fades as dominant driver shifts to fundamentals
strategic.unwind_trigger: BOJ delivers no hikes by September AND Iran re-escalates, driving both CHF short-squeeze and JPY long-unwind
strategic.historical_analogue: August 2024 carry-trade unwind: JPY positioning extremes reversed violently when BOJ hiked unexpectedly, though the current setup has less extreme JPY positioning
strategic.contrarian_case: Over 6 months, the contrarian case diminishes as positioning normalises and the dominant strategic driver shifts from positioning dynamics to fundamentals. A sustained BOJ disappointment combined with Iran re-escalation could generate a positioning-driven CHF/JPY rally toward 210+, but the probability of this joint outcome is estimated at 15-20% over a 6-month window.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Net commodity exposure and ToT asymmetry is the single biggest bilateral driver (+0.03 tactical / +0.02 strategic)

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral external balance comparison is heavily asymmetric. Switzerland runs a 7% current-account surplus (~CHF 70bn/yr of structural inflows) while Japan's current account is approximately +4% of GDP but facing trade-balance deterioration from energy imports. The net commodity exposure is decisive for CHF/JPY: using the framework's direction rules, oil up is mildly negative CHF (indirect through EUR weakness) but devastatingly negative JPY (Japan imports 95.9% of crude from ME). Net effect of oil at $102: strongly bearish JPY relative to CHF, which has driven the pair to 204. However, the CHANGE in oil (from $120 peak toward $102, projected $80 on ceasefire) is the tactical signal — and the change is JPY-positive, hence bearish CHF/JPY. Bilateral trade data shows Swiss merchandise exports to Japan at CHF 7.5bn, with Switzerland being Japan's seventh-largest foreign investor. In 2023, bilateral trade volume totalled CHF 14.5bn with a modest Swiss surplus of CHF 0.7bn, while Japanese firms invested USD 41.8bn in Switzerland versus Swiss investment of USD 14.7bn in Japan. The bilateral investment imbalance (Japan investing 2.8x more in Switzerland) creates a structural CHF-supportive flow, but this is slow-burn and not tactically relevant.
key_bilateral_metrics: Swiss C/A surplus: ~7% of GDP vs Japan C/A: ~4% (but deteriorating on energy)
Bilateral trade volume: ~CHF 14.5bn (modest; not a dominant flow)
Japanese FDI in Switzerland: USD 41.8bn vs Swiss FDI in Japan: USD 14.7bn
Oil price trajectory: $102 (falling from $120 on ceasefire) — bearish CHF/JPY
Net commodity exposure for CHF/JPY: oil down = strongly bearish for pair (JPY recovers faster)
tactical.what_is_priced: The Iran-war energy premium in CHF/JPY is approximately 15-20 yen above pre-war equilibrium. Ceasefire-driven oil normalisation toward $80 would erase most of this premium.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, if Iran ceasefire holds and Brent reverts to $80-85, Japan's terms of trade recover meaningfully while Switzerland's external position is largely unaffected (pharma/services exports are oil-insensitive). The external-balance differential narrows on the JPY side: Japan's primary income surplus of ~¥45-50T annualised provides a structural cushion that dominates once the energy shock abates. Switzerland's 7% C/A surplus remains the highest-quality structural flow in G10, but the CHANGE in relative external position favours JPY recovery. Gold trade flows through Switzerland (65-70% of global refining) at $4,700+ prices partially offset but are a slower channel.
key_bilateral_metrics: Projected Brent 6M: $80-90 (ceasefire base case)
Japan ToT recovery potential: significant (from -35% shock toward equilibrium)
Swiss C/A trajectory: stable at ~7%
Japan primary income 6M projection: ~¥22-25T (structural cushion)
Gold trade impulse through Switzerland: +30% above trend (diminishing if gold consolidates)
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets partially price ToT recovery for Japan but the magnitude of CHF/JPY's energy premium suggests further downside is available if Brent breaks $90 sustainably.

factors.horizon_split: Both Bearish; strategic confidence slightly lower (60 vs 65) because the 6M horizon includes path-dependency on the Iran resolution pace.

net_commodity_exposure.base_commodity_profile: Switzerland: negligible direct commodity exposure; energy weight in CPI ~5%; pharma and services exports are oil-insensitive; gold refining (65-70% of global) benefits from elevated gold prices
net_commodity_exposure.quote_commodity_profile: Japan: extreme oil import dependency at 95.9% of crude from Middle East; energy weight in CPI ~20%; oil price is the dominant terms-of-trade driver; primary income surplus provides partial cushion
net_commodity_exposure.net_effect_calculation: Step 1: Oil up $10 — CHF mildly negative (indirect EUR weakness channel, ~0.1-0.2% CHF depreciation vs USD) while JPY severely negative (direct ToT deterioration, ~0.5-1.0% JPY depreciation vs USD). Net CHF/JPY effect: oil up = CHF strengthens vs JPY = CHF/JPY rises. Step 2: Oil down $10 — CHF mildly positive (minor energy-cost benefit) while JPY strongly positive (ToT recovery, ~0.5-1.0% JPY appreciation vs USD). Net CHF/JPY effect: oil down = CHF weakens vs JPY = CHF/JPY falls. Step 3: Current trajectory — Brent falling from $120 to $102, targeting $80 on ceasefire — this is JPY-positive and bearish CHF/JPY. The magnitude of JPY sensitivity (~10x CHF sensitivity to oil) makes this factor decisively bearish for the pair as oil normalises.
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_up_10pct: CHF/JPY rallies 150-300 pips as JPY suffers disproportionate ToT deterioration versus CHF; safe-haven premium re-establishes
net_commodity_exposure.scenario_oil_down_10pct: CHF/JPY falls 200-400 pips as JPY recovers disproportionately through ToT improvement; energy-asymmetry premium erodes
net_commodity_exposure.dominant_commodity_driver: Brent crude — the single most important driver of CHF-vs-JPY safe-haven asymmetry for this pair, given Japan's 95.9% ME crude dependence versus Switzerland's negligible energy import exposure

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The SNB and BOJ are moving in opposite directions communicatively. The SNB's March 19 MPA introduced explicit intervention escalation language ('willingness to intervene has increased'), which is CHF-bearish. The BOJ's April 28 meeting showed three dissenters for an immediate hike with Ueda explicitly stating 'real interest rates are at significantly low levels' — unambiguously JPY-bullish guidance. The bilateral guidance divergence is the widest it has been in the current cycle: SNB trying to cap CHF strength while the BOJ is signalling its intention to normalise further. This divergence is bearish CHF/JPY because both central banks' guidance pushes the pair lower. The SNB's credibility on intervention is partially constrained by US Treasury currency-manipulator scrutiny, while the BOJ's credibility on normalisation has been enhanced by the unprecedented three-way hawkish dissent.
key_bilateral_metrics: SNB MPA March 19: explicit 'willingness to intervene increased'
BOJ April 28 vote: 6-3 (3 dissenters for hike to 1.00%)
Next SNB meeting: June 18 (hold expected, ~95% probability)
Next BOJ meeting: June 16-17 (35-40% hike probability)
Guidance divergence direction: both central banks pushing CHF/JPY lower
tactical.what_is_priced: The guidance divergence is partially priced but the June 16-17/18 meeting cluster creates a binary event window where both central banks act in the same 48-hour window.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, the BOJ's normalisation path (2-3 meetings, potential 2 hikes) versus the SNB's anchored-to-easing path creates a sustained guidance divergence that compounds against CHF/JPY. Ueda's term runs to April 2028 with no personnel risk, while Schlegel's intervention rhetoric may need to transition to action if CHF stays strong, potentially introducing negative rates by September — both legs bearish CHF/JPY.
key_bilateral_metrics: BOJ 6M hike count (MUFG): 1-2 hikes (to 1.00-1.25%)
SNB 6M path: hold 0.00% (25-40% negative-rate probability by Q4)
Meeting calendar overlap: June 16-18, September 19/25 (BOJ/SNB clusters)
BOJ credibility trajectory: strengthening
SNB credibility trajectory: tested if EUR/CHF breaks 0.90
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets under-price the BOJ's conviction to normalise given the 6-3 vote. The SNB's intervention constraint (US Treasury scrutiny) is also under-priced.

factors.horizon_split: Both Bearish; strategic confidence higher (65 vs 60) because multi-meeting normalisation path provides sustained guidance divergence.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The Iran ceasefire trajectory is the dominant bilateral event risk. A ceasefire that sticks is unambiguously bearish CHF/JPY: it removes the energy-asymmetry premium that has been the pair's primary driver since February 2026. The May 1 MoF yen intervention (~¥5.4T) demonstrated a credible 160 ceiling on USD/JPY, which translates to downside pressure on JPY crosses including CHF/JPY. The BOJ Summary of Opinions release (expected ~May 7-8), the May 23 Tokyo CPI, and Q1 Japan GDP (May 19) are all tactically relevant for the JPY leg. On the CHF side, the Swiss-EU Bilaterals III debate and UBS regulatory reform are background noise with minimal 2-4 week impact.
key_bilateral_metrics: Iran ceasefire probability (2-4 weeks): ~50-60%
MoF intervention ceiling: USD/JPY ~160 (translates to CHF/JPY downside pressure)
Key CHF event: Swiss PPI May 13
Key JPY events: Q1 GDP May 19, Tokyo CPI May 23
Bilateral political risk: minimal (Switzerland-Japan relations are excellent)
tactical.what_is_priced: The ceasefire probability is approximately priced. The BOJ June meeting is the larger event risk that markets are under-pricing for CHF/JPY specifically.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, multiple event windows create two-way risk for CHF/JPY. BOJ normalisation is JPY-positive (bearish pair) but Trump trade-policy risk (auto tariffs on Japan) is JPY-negative (bullish pair). US-China tensions are roughly neutral for the pair (both currencies safe havens). Net: balanced with high variance.
key_bilateral_metrics: 6M tail-risk events bullish CHF/JPY: Iran re-escalation, Trump auto tariffs on Japan
6M tail-risk events bearish CHF/JPY: BOJ hike, Iran ceasefire, US recession
Combined probability of significant CHF/JPY move: high, but direction uncertain
Bilateral political relationship: stable (JSFTEPA in force since 2009)
US-China tension impact: roughly neutral for CHF/JPY (both safe havens)

factors.horizon_split: Tactical Bearish, Strategic Neutral. The ceasefire catalyst dominates tactically; multiple offsetting events balance strategically.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Extreme fiscal differential (16% vs 250% debt/GDP) warrants modest boost (+0.01 tactical / +0.01 strategic)

tactical.bilateral_analysis: Switzerland's fiscal superiority over Japan is the most extreme bilateral fiscal differential in G10. Swiss federal debt-to-GDP at 16.1% versus Japan's gross debt-to-GDP at ~250% represents a 15x differential. Switzerland's AAA rating across all agencies versus Japan's A+/A1/A contrasts sharply. The Swiss balanced budget (2025 surplus of CHF 0.3bn) versus Japan's Takaichi stimulus (¥21.3T supplementary, ¥120T initial budget) reflects fundamentally different fiscal philosophies. This differential is structurally bullish CHF/JPY because it underpins the CHF safe-asset bid. However, Japan's fiscal dynamics are uniquely insulated by the BOJ's 50% JGB ownership, which suppresses the fiscal-risk channel that would normally weaken the currency. Net: bullish CHF/JPY but the BOJ-fiscal backstop caps the magnitude.
key_bilateral_metrics: Swiss federal debt/GDP: 16.1% vs Japan gross debt/GDP: ~250% (15x differential)
Swiss sovereign rating: AAA (all agencies) vs Japan: A+/A1/A
Swiss 2025 fiscal balance: +CHF 0.3bn surplus vs Japan: deficit ~8% of GDP
JGB 10Y: 2.50% vs Swiss 10Y: 0.35% = 215bp quality spread
BOJ JGB ownership: ~50% (backstop mechanism capping fiscal risk)
tactical.what_is_priced: The fiscal quality differential is well-known and largely priced. Tactical impact is minimal unless Japan faces an acute fiscal event.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, the fiscal differential could become more relevant if BOJ normalisation accelerates and JGB yields break 3%, testing the fiscal sustainability dynamics that the BOJ-backstop has suppressed. Each 100bp rise in average JGB yield costs ~2% of GDP in additional debt servicing, creating a potential feedback loop that constrains BOJ hawkishness. This second-order effect is mildly bullish CHF/JPY at the strategic horizon because it caps how far the BOJ can normalise before fiscal constraints bite.
key_bilateral_metrics: Japan interest expense as % of revenue: ~22% (rising)
JGB rollover risk: low (domestic ownership ~90%)
Swiss fiscal trajectory: stable, balanced budgets through 2028
If JGB 10Y breaks 3%: fiscal headwind emerges for further BOJ hikes
Takaichi stimulus ongoing JGB issuance: continued supply pressure
strategic.what_is_priced: Markets price the fiscal-quality differential as structural. The second-order BOJ-constraint effect is under-appreciated and mildly bullish CHF/JPY.

factors.horizon_split: Both Bullish CHF/JPY; confidence high and aligned. This is the single strongest structural positive for CHF/JPY.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: CHF/JPY's trend regime is in the 93rd percentile of the 52-week range with MAs bullish-stacked (spot above 50d above 100d above 200d), but the convergence of 50d (201.58) and 100d (201.44) within 15 pips of spot signals momentum exhaustion. BabyPips analysis from April 14 noted CHF/JPY was 'stuck inside a range' with 'safe-haven flows and SNB jawboning' on the CHF side and 'BOJ rhetoric and currency intervention threats' on the JPY side. TradingView analysis identified a rising wedge pattern with a subsequent break below the support line and 'considerable bearish momentum.' Support levels: 200.00-200.15 (pivot/round number), 198.44-198.57 (swing low), 195.85 (December support). Resistance: 202.71 (overlap resistance), 203.33-203.83 (supply zone), 204.43 (52-week high). The pair's realised volatility has compressed from war-peak levels, and the range-bound structure suggests consolidation ahead of the June central bank cluster.
key_bilateral_metrics: 52-week high: 204.43; 52-week low: 166.09; range percentile: 93rd
Estimated 1M IV: 8-10%; estimated 20d RV: 7-9%
tactical.what_is_priced: The range-bound consolidation structure is well-priced. No significant mispricing identified in the vol landscape.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the 200d SMA at 198.10 is the key structural level. A sustained break below 200 would confirm a new downtrend; holding above 200 maintains the bullish-stacked regime. The 200d SMA has provided support on every test since January 2026.
key_bilateral_metrics: 200d SMA: 198.10 (key structural support)
Strategic range expectation: 192-208
MA alignment: bullish-stacked but momentum fading
Vol regime: normalising, 6M IV expected 8-10%
Range-bound consolidation pending June binary catalyst
strategic.what_is_priced: Strategic options price a wide range, consistent with the binary BOJ/Iran catalysts.

factors.horizon_split: Both Neutral; the range-bound structure provides a context for directional views from other factors without itself generating a signal.

expression_assessment.vol_landscape_summary: CHF/JPY vol regime is transitional — compressed from wartime peaks (daily ranges 2-3 yen) to current 1.0-1.5 yen daily ranges; estimated 1M IV 8-10% with range-bound consolidation near 52-week highs ahead of the June 16-18 BOJ/SNB binary cluster
expression_assessment.directional_expression: The directional bearish view is best expressed via spot short CHF/JPY with a stop above 204.50 (52-week high). Options expression is less efficient due to the cross-pair nature of CHF/JPY (less liquid than major pairs). For event risk around the June 16-18 central bank cluster, short-dated CHF/JPY puts (or equivalent synthetic) would capture the binary BOJ/SNB outcome, but liquidity constraints on CHF/JPY options favour the spot expression.
expression_assessment.event_timing_consideration: The June 16-17 BOJ MPM and June 18 SNB MPA create a 48-hour binary cluster that dominates expression choice. Positioning ahead of this cluster by May 23 (Tokyo CPI) allows adjustment based on the hike probability signal. Short-dated options expiring post-June 18 capture both events but suffer from cross-pair liquidity costs.
expression_assessment.liquidity_note: CHF/JPY is a liquid cross pair but less so than major pairs; typical bid/offer spreads of 2-5 pips in spot, wider in options. Execution via spot is preferred for position sizes up to $50M equivalent; larger positions may require breaking into tranches or using CHF/USD and USD/JPY legs separately.

factors.weight_adjustment_reason: Non-USD cross; vehicle-currency channel is neutral, reducing relevance (-0.05 tactical / -0.05 strategic)

tactical.bilateral_analysis: For CHF/JPY as a cross pair, USD liquidity operates through the vehicle currency channel: both CHF and JPY are traded primarily versus USD, so USD funding conditions affect both legs similarly. The Fed at 3.75% with a dovish bias provides stable-to-easing USD conditions, which supports both safe-haven currencies proportionally. The cross-currency basis for CHF/JPY specifically is driven by the differential between CHF/USD basis and JPY/USD basis — both structurally negative (both are funding currencies), with the JPY basis typically more negative. The net CHF/JPY basis effect is approximately neutral. The dollar smile dynamic is also roughly neutral for CHF/JPY: in a USD-weakening environment (bottom of smile), both CHF and JPY strengthen versus USD, but the cross effect depends on which strengthens more — currently CHF has been the stronger performer due to energy insulation.
key_bilateral_metrics: Fed funds: 3.75% (dovish bias)
CHF/USD basis: structurally negative (~-20 to -30bp)
JPY/USD basis: structurally more negative (~-30 to -50bp)
Net CHF/JPY basis effect: approximately neutral to mildly bullish (JPY funding slightly more expensive)
Dollar smile position: bottom-to-centre (both currencies benefit similarly)
tactical.what_is_priced: USD funding conditions are approximately neutral for CHF/JPY.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Over 6 months, the vehicle-currency channel continues to operate neutrally for the cross. As the Fed eases and the BOJ hikes, the JPY/USD basis should compress faster than the CHF/USD basis, creating a mild structural tailwind for JPY (bearish CHF/JPY) through the funding channel. However, the magnitude is small (10-20bp basis change) relative to the dominant rate-differential and safe-haven dynamics.
key_bilateral_metrics: Projected Fed funds end-2026: 3.25-3.50% (one cut)
JPY/USD basis compression projected: 10-20bp
CHF/USD basis trajectory: stable
Net CHF/JPY basis 6M: mildly bearish (favours JPY)
Cross-currency settlement: standard T+2 via USD vehicle
strategic.what_is_priced: Approximately neutral. The basis differential is too small to generate a meaningful signal.

factors.horizon_split: Both Neutral; USD liquidity is not a differentiating factor for this cross pair.

adjustments.reason: CHF/JPY is a safe-haven-vs-safe-haven cross where the energy-asymmetry regime makes risk appetite a dominant bilateral driver; increased by +0.03 tactical and +0.02 strategic
Net commodity exposure and ToT asymmetry is the single biggest bilateral driver given Japan's 95.9% ME crude dependence; increased by +0.03 tactical and +0.02 strategic
Non-USD cross; vehicle-currency channel is neutral for both safe-haven currencies, reducing relevance; decreased by -0.05 tactical and -0.05 strategic
Extreme fiscal differential (16% vs 250% debt/GDP) warrants modest boost; increased by +0.01 tactical and +0.01 strategic

tactical.score_interpretation: A net weighted score of -0.380 maps to a moderate Bearish CHF/JPY stance. Seven factors contribute negatively with Policy & Rates dominating at -0.156, one factor contributes positively (Fiscal at +0.038), and two are Neutral. The score falls in the -0.30 to -0.45 band, mapping to conviction 2/5 with moderate directional confidence. Conviction is capped at 2 rather than 3 because the binary Iran ceasefire outcome creates substantial near-term uncertainty — a re-escalation would violently reverse the thesis.
tactical.rationale: The tactical bearish CHF/JPY case rests on a convergence of cyclical catalysts working against the pair. First, Policy & Rates delivers the strongest signal: the BOJ's 6-3 hawkish dissent at the April 28 meeting creates a ~35-40% probability of a June 17 rate hike to 1.00%, while the SNB is anchored at 0.00% with intervention rhetoric that caps CHF upside — both legs push CHF/JPY lower. Second, Risk Appetite/Sentiment at the adjusted 0.13 weight captures the unwinding energy-asymmetry premium as Iran ceasefire negotiations progress: CHF's safe-haven premium over JPY has been the pair's dominant driver since February, and that premium is actively eroding. Third, External Balance at the adjusted 0.09 weight reflects the oil price trajectory (from $120 peak to $102, targeting $80) that disproportionately benefits JPY through terms-of-trade recovery. Fourth, bilateral positioning (specs short CHF and long JPY) aligns with downside for the pair. Against these, Fiscal & Sovereign is the sole bullish factor, reflecting Switzerland's AAA-rated fiscal fortress versus Japan's 250% debt/GDP — but this structural advantage is priced and operates slowly. The path of least resistance is a grind lower toward 198-200 over 2-4 weeks, with the June 16-17/18 BOJ/SNB meeting cluster as the decisive catalyst. The risk to the bearish thesis is a ceasefire breakdown with Brent spiking above $120, which would violently re-establish the energy-asymmetry premium and squeeze CHF/JPY back toward 204+. The net weighted score of -0.380 sits in the moderate-bearish zone with conviction 2/5, constrained by the binary outcome uncertainty on Iran and the structural CHF advantages that prevent a collapse scenario.
tactical.g10_relative_ranking: CHF/JPY ranks as the 2nd most attractive short among CHF crosses (after CHF vs high-carry currencies like AUD, NOK). Among JPY longs, the pair ranks 3rd behind USD/JPY and EUR/JPY shorts because the BOJ rate hike directly compresses those wider-differential pairs more efficiently. The cross is attractive for expressing the BOJ-normalisation theme without taking a USD view.

conditions: Iran ceasefire holds fragily with partial progress in negotiations
Brent stabilises at $95-100 (no dramatic move in either direction)
BOJ Summary of Opinions confirms hawkish momentum but no June hike surprise delivered yet
base_case.path: CHF/JPY drifts from 201.63 to 198-200 in a choppy grind lower within a 198-204 corridor; consolidation around 200-202 through May 20, then gradual drift toward 198-200 as Tokyo CPI and Q1 GDP frame the June BOJ decision

conditions: Iran ceasefire fails with Brent spiking above $120
Safe-haven energy-asymmetry premium re-establishes with CHF dramatically outperforming JPY
BOJ delivers surprise June hike to 1.00% with 7-2 or greater majority
Iran ceasefire firms with Brent toward $85 sustained

tactical.path_expectation: The 2-4 week path is most likely a choppy grind lower within a 198-204 corridor. Phase 1 (now through May 20): consolidation around 200-202 as markets digest Iran headlines and await Q1 Japan GDP on May 19. Phase 2 (May 20-June 10): gradual drift lower toward 198-200 as Tokyo CPI on May 23 and ECB on June 4 frame the June BOJ decision; a Tokyo CPI print above 2% would accelerate the move. Phase 3 (June 10-18): binary resolution around the BOJ June 16-17 and SNB June 18 cluster — a BOJ hike would drop CHF/JPY 200-300 pips in a single session, while a dovish hold would trigger a corrective bounce toward 202-203. Daily ranges expected 100-200 pips with spike-and-fade episodes on Iran headlines. The 200.00 round number and 200d SMA at 198.10 are the key gravitational levels.

increase_conditions: Increase to 4 if BOJ Summary of Opinions shows 4+ members supporting June hike AND Brent breaks $90 sustainably
Increase to 3 if Tokyo CPI prints above 2% headline OR Iran ceasefire framework deal signed
decrease_conditions: Decrease to 1 if Iran ceasefire fails AND Brent $120 sustained for 5+ days
Decrease to 0 (reverse to Bullish) if BOJ June meeting delivers dovish hold with 8-1 vote AND Brent $130

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Moderately favourable. The downside case loss is contained by the 204.43 52-week high acting as a ceiling, while the upside case (for the short) benefits from the June BOJ catalyst providing a potential 763-pip gain to 194.

strategic.score_interpretation: The net strategic score of -0.298 maps to a mild Bearish stance but with low conviction, placing it at the Bearish/Neutral boundary. Three factors contribute negatively (Policy & Rates at -0.168, External Balance at -0.108, Guidance at -0.078), one contributes positively (Fiscal at +0.056), and six are Neutral. The high number of Neutral factors reflects genuine offsetting bilateral dynamics: CHF's structural advantages (fiscal quality, current-account surplus, low inflation) counterbalance the cyclical JPY-positive forces (BOJ normalisation, ToT recovery). Conviction 2/5 at the Neutral boundary.
strategic.rationale: The strategic CHF/JPY picture is more balanced than the tactical view because the structural CHF positives reassert over longer horizons. Policy & Rates remains the dominant negative (-0.168) as the BOJ normalisation path widens the rate gap from -75bp toward -100bp to -125bp over 6 months. External Balance (-0.108) reflects the expected oil normalisation and Japan's ToT recovery. Guidance & Credibility (-0.078) captures the persistent BOJ hawkish trajectory versus SNB anchoring or easing. Against these, Fiscal & Sovereign (+0.056) is the countervailing positive — Switzerland's AAA-rated fiscal fortress versus Japan's 250% debt/GDP provides a structural floor for CHF/JPY that limits how far the pair can fall even if the BOJ normalises fully. The key insight is that the BOJ's normalisation itself is constrained by Japan's fiscal dynamics: each 100bp of JGB yield rise costs ~2% of GDP in additional servicing, potentially capping the BOJ's terminal rate and thus limiting CHF/JPY downside. Six factors are strategically Neutral because the safe-haven character of both currencies converges once the energy-asymmetry regime fades, leaving risk sentiment, positioning, and event risk broadly balanced. Exchange Rates UK aggregated bank forecasts project CHF/JPY softening to 174.41 by late 2026, while CoinCodex forecasts a 2026 range between 196.75 and 207.90 with an average of 201.07. Our view sits between these extremes: CHF/JPY likely trades 192-205 over 6 months with a mild downward bias toward 195-198 by November, driven by BOJ normalisation compounding against the SNB anchor but cushioned by CHF's structural fiscal and current-account advantages.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: Strategically, CHF/JPY short ranks 3rd among JPY long expressions (behind USD/JPY and EUR/JPY shorts, which have wider rate differentials). Among CHF shorts, CHF/JPY ranks 1st because the BOJ provides the most credible rate-convergence path of any G10 central bank.

conditions: BOJ delivers one hike to 1.00% by July/August
Iran ceasefire holds and Brent normalises to $85
SNB holds at 0.00% through year-end without signalling negative rates
Fed cuts once to 3.50% (broadly neutral for CHF/JPY cross)
base_case.path: Phased decline: range-trade 198-204 pending June BOJ/SNB meetings, directional move lower in July-August if BOJ hike delivers, targeting 195-198 with second compression toward 192-195 possible if second BOJ hike materialises by September. Volatility expected to decline as energy-asymmetry regime fades.

conditions: Iran ceasefire fails with Brent sustaining above $120
BOJ holds on growth concerns, abandoning normalisation path
BOJ delivers two hikes to 1.25% by September and signals further tightening
Iran fully resolves with Brent breaking below $80 and SNB signals negative-rate possibility

strategic.path_expectation: The 6M path is a phased decline interrupted by consolidation windows. Phase 1 (May-June): range-trade 198-204 pending BOJ/SNB June 16-18 meetings — this is the decisive binary window. Phase 2 (July-August): directional move lower if BOJ delivers June or July hike, targeting 195-198 as the rate differential widens from -75bp toward -100bp. Phase 3 (September-November): further compression toward 192-195 if a second BOJ hike materialises and Iran resolution sticks, with Brent sub-$85 confirming Japan's ToT recovery. Volatility expected to decline from wartime elevated levels as the energy-asymmetry regime fades, with daily ranges compressing from 1.0-1.5 yen to 0.7-1.2 yen. The 200d SMA at 198.10 (rising to approximately 199-200 by August) is the key structural pivot separating the base case from the downside case.

increase_conditions: Increase to 4 if BOJ delivers two hikes by September AND Brent sustained below $85
Increase to 3 if BOJ delivers June hike AND Iran ceasefire firms with Brent sub-$90
decrease_conditions: Decrease to 1 if BOJ disappoints (no hike by September) AND Brent $100 sustained
Decrease to 0 (reverse to Bullish) if SNB cuts to negative rates AND Iran re-escalates with Brent $130

risk_reward.asymmetry_assessment: Favourable. The downside-case probability (Iran re-escalation) at 20% is lower than the upside-case probability (BOJ normalisation succeeds) at 30%, creating a positive skew with expected value of +503 pips over 6 months.

consensus_divergence.bank_consensus_forecast: Exchange Rates UK aggregated bank forecasts project CHF/JPY at 182.90 in 1 month, 179.86 in 3 months, and 175.67 in 6 months — implying a dramatic ~13% decline from current levels [1]
consensus_divergence.our_tactical_view: Bearish, conviction 2/5; target CHF/JPY 198-200 over 2-4 weeks (~1-2% lower)
consensus_divergence.our_strategic_view: Neutral-to-mild-Bearish, conviction 2/5; target CHF/JPY 195-198 by November 2026 (~2-3% lower)
consensus_divergence.divergence_direction: above consensus (we are materially less bearish than consensus)
consensus_divergence.divergence_magnitude: +2000+ pips above consensus at 6M (we see 195-198 vs consensus 175.67)
consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: The consensus forecasts appear to have been compiled before the Iran war's impact on CHF/JPY and are based on pre-war rate-differential models that do not account for Switzerland's structural fiscal and current-account advantages. We weight Switzerland's 7% current-account surplus, AAA sovereign rating, and the structural CHF safe-haven floor more heavily than pure rate-differential models suggest. The CHF premium will persist even after Iran resolves because Switzerland's fiscal fortress (16% debt/GDP versus Japan's 250%) and superior external balance provide a floor that mechanistic rate models miss. The consensus also likely over-extrapolates the BOJ normalisation path without accounting for Japan's fiscal constraint on terminal rate.
consensus_divergence.payoff_if_right: Short CHF/JPY positioned at 201.63 targeting 195-198 generates 365-566 pips (~1.8-2.8%) over 6 months; significantly better risk-adjusted return than consensus-chasing toward 175
consensus_divergence.risk_if_wrong: If consensus is right (CHF/JPY 175), we would have missed a 2600+ pip move; more likely scenario where we are wrong is CHF/JPY holds 198-205 on Iran re-escalation and BOJ disappointment

cross_horizon_consistency.explanation: The tactical (Bearish, -0.380) and strategic (Neutral-to-mild-Bearish, -0.298) views are directionally aligned but the strategic view is weaker. Both horizons are driven by the same fundamental forces — BOJ normalisation versus SNB anchoring — but the strategic view acknowledges the countervailing structural CHF positives (fiscal, current account) that reassert over longer horizons. The difference in net scores (-0.082) reflects six strategic factors moving to Neutral from their tactical bearish positions as the energy-asymmetry regime fades.
cross_horizon_consistency.inflection_point: June 16-18 BOJ/SNB meeting cluster: a BOJ hike would confirm the tactical bearish thesis and potentially upgrade strategic conviction to 3/5; a BOJ dovish hold would weaken both horizons
cross_horizon_consistency.trading_implication: Position tactically for CHF/JPY downside with tight stops above 204.50 (52-week high), and be prepared to add strategic positions after the June cluster resolves; do not front-run the strategic view with full position before the June binary is resolved

forward_vs_spot.forward_1m: ~201.50 (-13 pips); implied move: -0.8% annualised (CHF depreciating mildly)
forward_vs_spot.edge_3m: +125 to +525 pips (we expect significantly more CHF/JPY decline than the -38 pip forward implies)

catalysts.rationale: First full-month data reflecting Iran-war energy import surge. A weaker-than-expected trade balance confirms terms-of-trade damage from elevated crude costs, transmitting to JPY through the external balance channel. However the data is backward-looking; current account surplus resilience (primary income cushion of ~¥45-50T annualised) would support JPY and reinforce bearish CHF/JPY pressure. A worse-than-expected deficit would be ambiguous — confirming ToT damage but also reducing JPY safe-haven appeal.

Weak Q1 GDP could signal BOJ caution and reduce June hike probability, which would be mildly bullish CHF/JPY. However the weak growth is already embedded in the BOJ's April Outlook Report 0.5% FY2026 GDP forecast, and the BOJ has explicitly prioritised inflation over growth in recent communications. The bilateral transmission is therefore muted: a significant miss (negative GDP) could shift the JPY leg bearish but the effect would be modest relative to the ceasefire and BOJ rate catalysts.

A Tokyo CPI print above 2% headline would significantly increase June BOJ hike probability from ~35-40% toward 55-65%, directly strengthening JPY and pressuring CHF/JPY lower by an estimated 100-200 pips on the day. Tokyo CPI leads national CPI and is the BOJ's most watched near-term inflation signal. Consensus expects ~1.5-1.7%; an upside surprise above 2.0% is the most important near-term catalyst for the pair, representing a clean bilateral bearish signal for CHF/JPY through the JPY leg.

A hawkish ECB widens the EUR-CHF rate gap (bearish CHF through EUR/CHF appreciation), but also signals global stagflation persistence that supports all safe-haven currencies including JPY. The net bilateral effect on CHF/JPY is approximately neutral: CHF leg affected through EUR/CHF cross-currency, JPY leg affected through global risk sentiment. An unexpected ECB cut would be mildly bullish CHF/JPY (EUR/CHF lower, CHF stronger) but the magnitude is secondary to the BOJ/SNB bilateral drivers.

Three dissenters at the April 28 meeting create ~35-40% probability of a June hike to 1.00%. A hike would drop CHF/JPY 200-300 pips in a single session as the bilateral rate gap widens from -75bp to -100bp, directly validating the rate-divergence thesis. Even a hold with maintained 3-way dissent would preserve momentum toward a July hike and keep CHF/JPY under pressure. A dovish hold (6-3 reverts to near-unanimity) would be the single most bearish outcome for the CHF/JPY short thesis, potentially triggering a 100-200 pip corrective bounce. This is the single most important catalyst for the pair in the 2-4 week window.

A hold with maintained intervention rhetoric is the base case (~60% probability) and is mildly bullish CHF/JPY as it reaffirms the SNB's CHF-capping stance without action. A negative-rate signal would be sharply bearish CHF (and thus bearish CHF/JPY) with a potential 200-400 pip drop. The SNB meeting 24 hours after the BOJ creates a bilateral binary event cluster — if BOJ hikes (bearish CHF/JPY) and SNB signals negative rates (also bearish CHF/JPY), the combined 48-hour move could be 400-700 pips lower. The SNB's credibility is partially constrained by US Treasury currency-manipulator scrutiny.

catalysts.event: BOJ MPM with Quarterly Outlook Report
catalysts.rationale: If June was a hold, July becomes the dominant hike candidate with the quarterly Outlook Report providing the multi-meeting normalisation framework. A July hike to 1.00% would drop CHF/JPY 150-300 pips as markets price 2 hikes into the year-end bilateral rate differential. The Outlook Report's inflation and growth projections will set the pace of normalisation for H2 2026, directly affecting the strategic bilateral rate-differential trajectory. This is the most important strategic catalyst after the June cluster.

catalysts.event: BOJ MPM / SNB MPA September Cluster
catalysts.rationale: By September, the BOJ may be at 1.00%+ and the SNB faces the critical decision on negative rates as EUR/CHF dynamics and Swiss CPI trajectory become clearer. A September BOJ hike to 1.25% combined with SNB hold creates the widest bilateral rate gap of the cycle (-125bp), strongly bearish CHF/JPY. An SNB negative-rate signal (possible if EUR/CHF breaks 0.90 or Swiss CPI goes negative) would add further bearish CHF/JPY pressure through both legs simultaneously. The September cluster is the most consequential strategic catalyst for the 6-month horizon.

invalidators.condition: CHF/JPY breaks above 207 and holds for 3 consecutive trading days (new multi-decade high confirmed)
invalidators.mechanism: Confirms the energy-asymmetry regime has re-established with Iran re-escalation; JPY safe-haven failure becomes structural not cyclical; technical breakout above 52-week high creates new upside momentum

invalidators.condition: BOJ delivers a dovish hold at June 17 with 8-1 vote AND removes 'real rates significantly low' language from the statement
invalidators.mechanism: The hawkish-pivot thesis is invalidated; BOJ normalisation path is extended or abandoned; CHF/JPY reverts to a range-trade driven by Iran outcome rather than rate convergence; bilateral rate differential stays at -75bp indefinitely
invalidators.monitor_indicator: BOJ June 17 policy statement and vote breakdown

invalidators.condition: Brent crude breaks $130 sustained for 5 consecutive trading days following confirmed ceasefire failure
invalidators.mechanism: Japan's ToT collapse re-establishes the energy-asymmetry premium that drove CHF/JPY from 166 to 204; JPY weakens dramatically on crude import cost surge while CHF's negligible energy exposure maintains safe-haven premium; pair targets 207-210+
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Brent crude daily closing price and Iran geopolitical news flow

invalidators.condition: Swiss CPI prints 1.5% YoY or higher for two consecutive monthly releases
invalidators.mechanism: Eliminates CHF's real-yield premium and creates SNB normalisation expectations; shifts the bilateral rate-gap trajectory in CHF's favour; markets would reprice SNB hike probability from 0% toward 20-30%, creating CHF upside relative to JPY
invalidators.monitor_indicator: Swiss Federal Statistical Office monthly CPI release

invalidators.condition: JGB 10Y yield breaks 3.00% sustained for 5 consecutive trading days AND BOJ explicitly signals fiscal concerns limit further hikes
invalidators.mechanism: Caps the rate-convergence thesis that is bearish CHF/JPY; BOJ fiscal constraint at 3% JGB yield prevents further rate hikes, removing the primary driver of CHF/JPY downside; simultaneously signals fiscal risk that could paradoxically weaken JPY if bond market instability emerges
invalidators.monitor_indicator: JGB 10Y daily yield and BOJ communications

signposts.confirms_if: Hike to 1.00% delivered by July MPM (confirms bilateral rate-divergence trajectory)
signposts.invalidates_if: No hike by September MPM with dovish language replacing 'real rates significantly low'
signposts.bilateral_importance: The single most direct driver of the CHF/JPY rate differential; each 25bp BOJ hike widens the bilateral gap from -75bp, reducing the pair's carry advantage for CHF longs and providing a fundamental anchor for JPY strength that directly pressures CHF/JPY lower

signposts.confirms_if: Sustained below $90 for 10+ consecutive trading days (ceasefire-driven normalisation)
signposts.invalidates_if: Sustained above $120 for 5+ consecutive trading days (re-escalation confirmed)
signposts.bilateral_importance: The single biggest driver of CHF-vs-JPY safe-haven asymmetry for this specific pair; Japan's 95.9% ME crude dependence means every $10 change in Brent has approximately 10x the ToT impact on JPY relative to CHF; lower oil directly strengthens JPY relative to CHF and is therefore the most bilateral-specific commodity signal available

signposts.confirms_if: Compresses below 170bp (JGB rising toward UST or UST falling)
signposts.invalidates_if: Re-widens above 220bp (JGB normalisation stalls or UST rises further)
signposts.bilateral_importance: Proxy for JPY strength dynamics that spill into CHF/JPY via the USD vehicle currency; JGB yield rising relative to UST signals BOJ normalisation progressing which directly strengthens JPY and pressures CHF/JPY lower; compression of this spread is the most liquid real-time signal for the JPY leg of the bilateral

signposts.confirms_if: Spot breaks below 200d SMA (198.10) and holds below for 5+ consecutive trading days
signposts.invalidates_if: Spot bounces off 200d SMA and rallies above 203 (confirming bullish-stacked regime intact)
signposts.bilateral_importance: Key structural support for the CHF/JPY pair; the 200d SMA has provided support on every test since January 2026; a sustained break below signals a regime change from the Iran-war bullish trend to a new bearish trend consistent with the BOJ-normalisation and ceasefire thesis, providing a technical confirmation of the fundamental bearish view

signposts.confirms_if: Maintains 40,000-80,000 net long range (durable accumulation supporting JPY strength)
signposts.invalidates_if: Surges above +100,000 (overcrowded long, squeeze risk) OR reverts to net short below zero (structural JPY bearishness re-establishes)
signposts.bilateral_importance: JPY positioning sustainability directly affects CHF/JPY downside durability; the current +56,400 leveraged-fund long is the dominant flow supporting the bearish CHF/JPY thesis — if positioning holds in the 40-80k range, the structural flow bias toward CHF/JPY downside persists; if it surges above 100k, the squeeze risk becomes the dominant risk for the pair rather than the fundamental downside

assumptions: CHF/JPY forward points estimated from the -75bp bilateral rate differential; precise forward outrights may differ slightly due to cross-currency basis and bid/offer spreads not captured in the simplified calculation.

Pair-specific implied volatility estimated from component-currency IV (USD/CHF ~7-8%, USD/JPY ~9-10%) using correlation assumptions; precise CHF/JPY IV not directly retrievable from public sources at time of analysis.

Risk reversal data for CHF/JPY specifically not available; characterised directionally from component-currency risk reversals (CHF-call bid on USD/CHF, JPY-call bid on USD/JPY) implying mild put demand on CHF/JPY.
Bilateral trade and FDI data from SJCC/SCCIJ (2023 vintage); more recent bilateral data not publicly available at time of writing.
Weight adjustments reflect the unique safe-haven-vs-safe-haven cross character where energy-asymmetry (Factors 3 and 5) dominates and USD liquidity (Factor 10) is less relevant for a non-USD cross.
PPP comparison uses the CHF dossier's estimate of CHF/JPY fair value at 195-205, approximately aligned with current spot of 201.63.

The BOJ June hike probability of 35-40% is derived from the dossier's assessment of the 6-3 vote and Ueda's 'real rates significantly low' language; precise OIS-implied pricing not retrieved from live data.
Iran ceasefire status is highly fluid; analysis reflects the May 6-9, 2026 information set and may become stale rapidly on geopolitical developments.
Brent crude at ~$102/bbl is used as the reference price; this reflects the post-wartime-peak partially-recovered level and is a critical assumption for the energy-asymmetry analysis.
